matches.matched_text: Old Finncoole, he's a mellow old saoul when he swills with his fuddlers free!
matches.snippet: Sing: Old Finncoole, he's a mellow old saoul when he swills with his fuddlers free! Poppop array!
matches.reasoning: Direct reference to Finnegans—Finncoole is a garbled/poetic variant of the Finnegan/Finn MacCool name; context of drinking, conviviality, and sociability evokes the ballad's wake-drinking motif

matches.matched_text: Big Maester Finnykin with Phenicia Parkes
matches.snippet: Big Maester Finnykin with Phenicia Parkes, lame of his ear and gape of her leg, most correctingly
matches.reasoning: Finnykin is a diminutive avatar of Finnegan/Finn; 'Maester' (master/builder/craftsman) echoes Bygmester Finnegan the hod-carrier; paired description of physical characteristics suggests the giant-figure motif

matches.snippet: Dik Gill, Tum Lung or Macfinnan's cool Harryng? He has only his hedcosycasket on
matches.reasoning: Macfinnan is a transparent garbling of MacCool/Mac-Cumhaill; 'cool' puns on both temperature and the MacCool element; context of sleeping giant with cosycasket reinforces the recumbent figure motif

matches.snippet: socks for he always must to insure warm sleep between a pair of fullyfleeced bankers like a finnoc in a cauwl
matches.reasoning: finnoc echoes Finn; comparison to a small fish (finnoc = Irish for small fish) wrapped in bedding (cauwl = cowl/enclosure) plays on Finn as sleeping giant cocooned in landscape

matches.snippet: D'Oyly Owens holds (though Finn Magnusson of himself holds also) that so long as there is a joint deposit account
matches.reasoning: Direct name Finn in legal/contractual context; appears as a legal authority figure, though this may reference an actual historical figure (Finn Magnusson was a real 19th-century scholar), the presence in this Joycean passage suggests intentional dual evocation
